section.title: The Yoke of Babylon and Hananiah
section.sectionRationale: Jeremiah wears an actual wooden yoke to dramatize submission to Babylon as God's appointed discipline, sending the same message to surrounding kings and to Zedekiah himself. The confrontation comes to a head when the prophet Hananiah publicly breaks that yoke and promises deliverance within two years, forcing the sharpest test yet of how a true word from God can be told from a comforting lie.
section.thesis: God has placed the whole region under Babylon's yoke for a set time, and any prophet, like Hananiah, who promises quick deliverance instead is prophesying a lie, so that breaking a wooden yoke only earns the nation an iron one, and death for the false prophet who broke it.
section.frameNote: Four scenes: the yoke of Babylon proclaimed to Judah and the surrounding nations, a warning about the temple vessels and false hopes of their quick return, Hananiah breaking Jeremiah's yoke before the people, and Jeremiah's grim reply of an iron yoke and Hananiah's death within the year.

verses.text: At the beginning of the reign of Zedekiah son of Josiah king of Judah, this word came to Jeremiah from the LORD.

This is what the LORD said to me: 
“Make for yourself a {{ol|yoke}} out of leather straps and put it on your neck.
Send word to the kings of Edom, Moab, Ammon, Tyre, and Sidon through the envoys who have come to Jerusalem to Zedekiah king of Judah.
Give them a message from the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, to relay to their masters:
By My great power and outstretched arm, I made the earth and the men and beasts on the face of it, and I give it to whom I please.
So now I have placed all these lands under the authority of My servant Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon. I have even made the beasts of the field subject to him.
All nations will serve him and his son and grandson, until the time of his own land comes; then many nations and great kings will enslave him.

As for the nation or kingdom that does not serve Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon and does not place its neck under his yoke, I will punish that nation by sword and famine and plague, declares the LORD, until I have destroyed it by his hand.
But as for you, do not listen to your prophets, your diviners, your interpreters of dreams, your mediums, or your sorcerers who declare, ‘You will not serve the king of Babylon.’
For they prophesy to you a {{sheqer|lie}} that will serve to remove you from your land; I will banish you and you will perish.
But the nation that will put its neck under the yoke of the king of Babylon and serve him, I will leave in its own land, to cultivate it and reside in it, declares the LORD.”
And to Zedekiah king of Judah I spoke the same message: “Put your necks under the yoke of the king of Babylon; serve him and his people, and live!
Why should you and your people die by sword and famine and plague, as the LORD has decreed against any nation that does not serve the king of Babylon?
Do not listen to the words of the prophets who say, ‘You must not serve the king of Babylon,’ for they are prophesying to you a lie.
For I have not sent them, declares the LORD, and yet they are prophesying falsely in My name; therefore I will banish you, and you will perish, you and the prophets who prophesy to you.”

units.label: The temple vessels and false hopes
units.note: not soon, but at God's appointed time

verses.text: Then I said to the priests and to all this people, “This is what the LORD says: Do not listen to the words of your prophets who prophesy to you, saying, ‘Look, very soon now the articles from the house of the LORD will be brought back from Babylon.’ They are prophesying to you a lie.
Do not listen to them. Serve the king of Babylon and live! Why should this city become a ruin?

If they are indeed prophets and the word of the LORD is with them, let them now plead with the LORD of Hosts that the articles remaining in the house of the LORD, in the palace of the king of Judah, and in Jerusalem, not be taken to Babylon.
For this is what the LORD of Hosts says about the pillars, the sea, the bases, and the rest of the articles that remain in this city,
which Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon did not take when he carried Jeconiah son of Jehoiakim king of Judah into exile from Jerusalem to Babylon, along with all the nobles of Judah and Jerusalem.
Yes, this is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says about the articles that remain in the house of the LORD, in the palace of the king of Judah, and in Jerusalem:
‘They will be carried to Babylon and will remain there until the day I attend to them again,’ declares the LORD. ‘Then I will bring them back and restore them to this place.’”

units.label: Hananiah breaks the yoke
units.note: a wooden yoke shattered in the LORD's house

verses.text: In the fifth month of that same year, the fourth year, near the beginning of the reign of King Zedekiah of Judah, the prophet Hananiah son of Azzur, who was from Gibeon, said to me in the house of the LORD in the presence of the priests and all the people:
“This is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: ‘I have broken the yoke of the king of Babylon.
Within two years I will restore to this place all the articles of the house of the LORD that Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon removed from here and carried to Babylon.

And I will restore to this place Jeconiah son of Jehoiakim king of Judah, along with all the exiles from Judah who went to Babylon,’ declares the LORD, ‘for I will break the yoke of the king of Babylon.’”
Then the prophet Jeremiah replied to the prophet Hananiah in the presence of the priests and all the people who were standing in the house of the LORD.
“Amen!” Jeremiah said. “May the LORD do so! May the LORD fulfill the words you have prophesied, and may He restore the articles of His house and all the exiles back to this place from Babylon.
Nevertheless, listen now to this message I am speaking in your hearing and in the hearing of all the people.
The prophets of old who preceded you and me prophesied war, disaster, and plague against many lands and great kingdoms.
As for the prophet who prophesies peace, only if the word of the prophet comes true will the prophet be recognized as one the LORD has truly sent.”
Then the prophet Hananiah took the yoke off the neck of Jeremiah the prophet and broke it.

And in the presence of all the people Hananiah proclaimed, “This is what the LORD says: ‘In this way, within two years I will break the yoke of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon off the neck of all the nations.’” 
At this, Jeremiah the prophet went on his way.

units.label: An iron yoke, and Hananiah's death
units.note: the truth of a prophet's word tested

verses.text: But shortly after Hananiah the prophet had broken the yoke off his neck, the word of the LORD came to Jeremiah:
“Go and tell Hananiah that this is what the LORD says: ‘You have broken a yoke of wood, but in its place you have fashioned a yoke of iron.’

For this is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: ‘I have put a yoke of iron on the neck of all these nations to make them serve Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, and they will serve him. I have even given him control of the beasts of the field.’”
Then the prophet Jeremiah said to the prophet Hananiah, “Listen, Hananiah! The LORD did not send you, but you have persuaded this people to trust in a lie.
Therefore this is what the LORD says: ‘I am about to remove you from the face of the earth. You will die this year because you have preached rebellion against the LORD.’”
And in the seventh month of that very year, the prophet Hananiah died.

ol.gloss: A yoke, the wooden crossbar laid on an animal's neck for labor or submission, worn literally by Jeremiah as a sign that Judah and the surrounding nations must submit to Babylon's rule as God's appointed discipline. Hananiah's breaking of this yoke was meant to picture liberation, but God turns it into a prophecy of even harsher bondage, a wooden yoke replaced by one of iron.

sheqer.gloss: A lie or falsehood, the word Jeremiah repeatedly uses for the message of the prophets promising quick deliverance from Babylon. The same word closes the Hananiah episode, 'you have persuaded this people to trust in a sheqer', showing that comforting words not actually sent by God are, however sincerely delivered, still a lie.

basic.context: Jeremiah wears an actual wooden yoke around his neck as a sign that Judah, along with the surrounding nations of Edom, Moab, Ammon, Tyre, and Sidon, must submit to Babylon's rule, since God has appointed Nebuchadnezzar as His servant for this season. When the prophet Hananiah publicly breaks that yoke and promises the exiles and temple vessels will return within two years, Jeremiah's initial hope, 'Amen, may the LORD do so,' gives way to a stark verdict: Hananiah's message was never from God, and he will die within the year for persuading the people to trust a lie.
basic.hermeneutics: This narrative gives a real, concrete test for discerning true from false prophecy, stated directly in 28:9, a peace-prophecy is proven true only when it actually comes to pass. The uncomfortable truth the passage refuses to soften is that a sincere, popular, hopeful message, delivered confidently in God's name and even by another recognized prophet, can still be entirely false, and following it leads only to greater bondage.
basic.originalLanguages: The ol, yoke, Jeremiah wears is a vivid, physical sign of submission, and its breaking by Hananiah is meant to dramatize liberation, but God turns the image against him, a wooden yoke replaced by iron, harsher bondage rather than freedom. Throughout, the message of the peace-prophets is repeatedly called sheqer, a lie, regardless of how sincerely or popularly it was delivered, showing that good intentions or popularity do not make a message true.
basic.typology: The yoke of submission to a foreign power that God calls His people to bear anticipates the New Testament's call to submit even to difficult, imposed authorities as part of God's sovereign ordering of history, Romans 13:1. Hananiah's confident but false prophecy anticipates the New Testament's repeated warnings about false teachers who speak comforting words that are never actually from God.
basic.christ: Christ invites His followers to take His yoke instead, Matthew 11:29-30, a yoke that, unlike Babylon's, is easy and light, the ultimate contrast to every burdensome and false yoke offered by human power or human deception. Where Hananiah offered a false, premature liberation that led only to harsher bondage, Christ offers a true and lasting rest that comes only through genuine submission to Him, the pattern this whole episode of false hope versus true submission anticipates.
basic.god: God is sovereign even over pagan empires, explicitly calling Nebuchadnezzar 'My servant' and giving him authority over nations by His own appointment, not merely allowing it. Yet God is also a God of truth who will not let a false message stand indefinitely, however sincerely or popularly proclaimed, holding false prophets accountable and vindicating His true word within the timeframe He sets.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 11:29-30 contrasts every burdensome yoke with Christ's own yoke, which is easy and light. Romans 13:1-2 teaches submission to governing authorities as part of God's sovereign ordering, echoing the call to bear Babylon's yoke as God's appointed discipline. Deuteronomy 18:21-22 gives the same test cited in Jeremiah 28:9, a prophet's word is proven true only by its fulfillment. 2 Peter 2:1-3 warns of false teachers exploiting people with fabricated messages, exactly Hananiah's pattern.
basic.oneStory: The yoke of Babylon, worn literally by Jeremiah and broken falsely by Hananiah, shows how tempting it is to trade God's hard but true word for a comforting lie, and how much heavier the bondage becomes when that lie is believed. That whole pattern of false, burdensome yokes finds its answer in Christ, who offers His people a yoke that is genuinely easy and a burden that is genuinely light, the true liberation Hananiah only pretended to offer.

context.literary: This section pairs a sustained sign act, Jeremiah wearing an actual yoke and sending its message to multiple nations, with a tense narrative confrontation between two prophets claiming to speak for the same LORD, creating one of the Old Testament's clearest test cases for discerning true from false prophecy.
context.historical: Set early in Zedekiah's reign, after the first deportation of 597 BC had already taken King Jeconiah, the nobles, and many temple articles to Babylon, this passage addresses the live political question of whether Judah, and its neighboring kingdoms, should attempt rebellion against Babylon or accept its rule as inevitable.
context.canonical: This episode gives Scripture's clearest narrated example of the test for a true prophet laid down in Deuteronomy 18:21-22, that a prophecy is proven true only by its actual fulfillment, playing out in real time as Hananiah's confident two-year prediction is answered instead by his own death within the year.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To demonstrate concretely, through the direct confrontation between Jeremiah and Hananiah, that a message delivered sincerely, popularly, and even by a recognized prophet in God's name can still be entirely false, and that submission to God's actual appointed discipline, however unwelcome, is the path to life rather than destruction.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is a specific historical narrative about a specific political and prophetic crisis; it does not prescribe submission to every foreign power in every circumstance as an unconditional rule. It does establish an enduring test for discerning true prophecy from false, fulfillment over time, verse 28:9, and it models genuine hope, 'Amen, may the LORD do so,' held together with sober discernment.

debates.question: Was Jeremiah wrong to initially respond 'Amen' to Hananiah's prophecy before later contradicting it?

views.label: A genuine, hopeful wish rather than an endorsement
views.case: Jeremiah's 'Amen, may the LORD do so' expresses his own personal longing for restoration to be true, immediately followed in the very same breath by a substantive test, verse 9, showing it was not an endorsement but an honest hope paired with careful discernment.

views.label: A rhetorical opening before the decisive rebuttal
views.case: By first acknowledging the desirability of Hananiah's message, Jeremiah disarms any accusation that he opposes restoration itself, making his subsequent correction land as a matter of truth, not personal preference or rivalry.

debates.bearing: Either way, the passage models that hoping for good news and testing that news against God's actual, prior revealed word are not in tension, both are held together in Jeremiah's response.

study.originalLanguages: The ol, yoke, Jeremiah wears is a vivid physical sign of imposed submission, and Hananiah's breaking of it is meant to picture imminent liberation, but God overturns the image entirely, promising a yoke of iron in place of wood, deeper bondage instead of freedom. Throughout the whole episode, the confident, popular message of peace is repeatedly named sheqer, a lie, regardless of how sincerely delivered or how badly the people wanted it to be true.

typology.type: The yoke of submission to Babylon as God's appointed discipline, Jeremiah 27:2-11
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the New Testament's call to submit even to difficult civil authorities as part of God's sovereign ordering, Romans 13:1-2, while ultimately giving way to the far better yoke Christ offers His people, Matthew 11:29-30.

typology.type: Hananiah's confidently delivered but false prophecy, Jeremiah 28:2-4
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the New Testament's repeated warnings against false teachers whose fabricated, comforting words are not actually from God, 2 Peter 2:1-3.

study.christ: Christ directly takes up the image of the yoke and inverts it for His own disciples, 'Take My yoke upon you... for My yoke is easy and My burden is light,' Matthew 11:29-30, the ultimate contrast to both Babylon's genuinely heavy yoke and Hananiah's false promise of an easy liberation that never came. Where Hananiah offered premature comfort that led only to harsher bondage, Christ offers real and lasting rest, but only through genuine submission to Him, exactly the pattern of true versus false relief this whole episode plays out in miniature.
study.god: God's sovereignty extends even over pagan empires; He explicitly calls Nebuchadnezzar 'My servant' and grants him authority by direct appointment, not mere permission, showing His rule reaches every nation, not Israel alone. Yet God is equally a God who will not indefinitely tolerate falsehood spoken in His name, holding Hananiah accountable within the very timeframe of his own false prediction, and proving His true word reliable even when it is the harder message to bear.

crossReferences.note: Gives the foundational test for a true prophet, that the fulfillment of the word proves whether the LORD truly spoke it, the very test Hananiah fails within the chapter.
Christ offers His own yoke, easy and light, in deliberate contrast to every burdensome or false yoke offered by human power or deception.
Teaches submission to governing authorities as part of God's sovereign ordering of history, echoing the call to accept Babylon's rule as God's appointed discipline.
Warns of false teachers who exploit people with fabricated words for their own gain, exactly Hananiah's pattern in this episode.

study.oneStory: The yoke of Babylon, worn literally by Jeremiah and falsely broken by Hananiah, exposes how easily people trade God's hard but true word for a comforting lie, and how much heavier the resulting bondage becomes. That entire pattern of false, burdensome yokes finds its resolution in Christ, whose yoke alone is genuinely easy and whose burden alone is genuinely light, the true and lasting liberation Hananiah only pretended to offer.

quiz.q: What does Jeremiah's wooden yoke symbolize in this passage?

options.text: Submission to Babylon's rule as God's appointed discipline for Judah and the surrounding nations.
options.note: God explicitly calls Nebuchadnezzar 'My servant' and commands submission to his rule for a set time.

options.text: A call to immediate armed rebellion against Babylon.
options.note: The message is the opposite, submit to Babylon's yoke rather than resist it.

options.text: A purely private symbol with no message for other nations.
options.note: The message is explicitly sent to the kings of Edom, Moab, Ammon, Tyre, and Sidon as well as Judah.

options.text: A sign that Judah's temple would never be destroyed.
options.note: The yoke concerns political submission to Babylon, not a promise about the temple's permanence.

quiz.q: What does Hananiah publicly do and prophesy in chapter 28?

options.text: He breaks Jeremiah's yoke and prophesies that within two years Babylon's rule and the exile will be over.
options.note: Verses 2 to 4 and 10 to 11 record both the symbolic act and the specific two-year timeframe.

options.text: He affirms Jeremiah's message and adds further warnings of judgment.
options.note: Hananiah directly contradicts Jeremiah's message, promising the opposite, quick deliverance.

options.text: He requests to see Jeremiah's yoke without commenting on its meaning.
options.note: He actively breaks the yoke and delivers a specific counter-prophecy.

options.text: He predicts a hundred years of continued Babylonian rule.
options.note: His specific prediction is a two-year timeframe for the yoke's end, not a hundred years.

quiz.q: What test does Jeremiah give for discerning a true prophet in verse 9?

options.text: A prophet who prophesies peace is recognized as truly sent by the LORD only if the word comes true.
options.note: This echoes the test given in Deuteronomy 18:21-22, fulfillment proves the source of the word.

options.text: A true prophet is recognized by how confidently he speaks.
options.note: Confidence of delivery is explicitly not the test; fulfillment is the standard given.

options.text: A true prophet is recognized by popular approval alone.
options.note: Hananiah appears to have had popular support, yet his message is proven false.

options.text: A true prophet must always prophesy disaster, never peace.
options.note: The text distinguishes prophets of old who spoke disaster from this specific test about peace-prophecies, not a blanket rule against ever prophesying peace.

quiz.q: What is the ultimate outcome of Hananiah breaking the wooden yoke?

options.text: God declares an iron yoke will replace the wooden one, and Hananiah himself dies within the year for preaching a lie.
options.note: Verses 13 to 17 record both the harsher judgment and Hananiah's specific death that same year.

options.text: The yoke of Babylon is permanently removed exactly as Hananiah predicted.
options.note: The opposite occurs; God announces an even harsher yoke of iron.

options.text: Jeremiah is punished instead of Hananiah for the broken yoke.
options.note: The judgment falls specifically and explicitly on Hananiah, not Jeremiah.

options.text: Nothing further happens; the incident is never resolved in the text.
options.note: Verse 17 explicitly records Hananiah's death that same year, resolving the confrontation.

quiz.q: How does Christ's teaching about His own yoke connect to this passage?

options.text: Christ offers His own yoke as easy and light, the ultimate contrast to Babylon's heavy yoke and Hananiah's false promise of easy liberation.
options.note: Matthew 11:29-30 deliberately uses the same yoke image in direct contrast to every burdensome or false yoke.

options.text: There is no connection between Christ's teaching and the yoke imagery in Jeremiah.
options.note: Matthew 11:29-30 uses the identical image of a yoke, inviting direct comparison.

options.text: Christ's yoke is described as even heavier than Babylon's.
options.note: Christ explicitly describes His yoke as easy and His burden as light, the opposite of heavier.

options.text: The connection is only that both involve farming equipment.
options.note: The connection is theological, submission and rest, not merely a shared agricultural tool.

questions: Hananiah's message was popular and comforting, but it was not from God. Where might you be drawn to a comforting message over a true one right now?

God called submission to Babylon's yoke, though painful, the path to life rather than destruction. Is there a hard circumstance you are resisting that God may actually be calling you to accept as His discipline?
Jeremiah's test for a true prophet was whether the word actually came true. How do you test the messages and promises you hear against God's revealed word rather than simply their appeal?
Hananiah's false liberation led only to a heavier yoke. Where have you seen a shortcut or comfortable lie ultimately cost more than the harder truth would have?
Christ offers His people a yoke that is genuinely easy and light. Where do you need to exchange a burden you are carrying on your own for genuine submission to Him?

translationNotes.note: 'Him and his son and grandson' reflects the actual succession of Babylonian kings after Nebuchadnezzar, showing this prophecy anticipated a limited, multi-generational span of Babylonian dominance rather than permanent rule.

Jeremiah's 'Amen! May the LORD do so!' expresses genuine longing for restoration to be true, not agreement with Hananiah's claim that it was already accomplished; the very next verses immediately qualify this with a substantive test of the prophecy's truth.

Hananiah's death 'in the seventh month of that very year' came just two months after his confrontation with Jeremiah in the fifth month, verse 1, a rapid and public vindication of Jeremiah's word within the same year.
